---
title: "Xorg Eating up my CPU"
date: 2008-07-07
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by FredJones on 2008-07-07
I have a GeForce 8400 GS and I used Envy to configure the drivers. top says that the process Xorg is using almost 40% of my CPU, a dual core 2G with 2G RAM and no other apps doing anything. Here is my /etc/X11/xorg.conf

```

# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 1.0  (buildd@vernadsky)  Tue Mar  4 20:24:34 UTC 2008

# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen      0  "Screen0" 0 0
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "BENQ G774"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 72.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 120.0
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Configured Video Device"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Videocard0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 8400 GS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "Configured Video Device"
    Monitor        "Configured Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "AddARGBGLXVisuals" "True"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "1"
    Option         "TwinViewXineramaInfoOrder" "CRT-0"
    Option         "metamodes" "CRT-0: nvidia-auto-select +0+0, CRT-1: nvidia-auto-select +1024+0"
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
    Option         "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection

```

any ideas? Brand new Hardy install

---

### Post by sdennie on 2008-07-07
Do you have any applications open at all?  Things like firefox and gnome-system-monitor tend to upset Xorg and make it use abnormal amounts of CPU.  I would try logging in, bringing up a terminal and typing top.  Then opening apps one by one to see if you can figure out which one is making Xorg unhappy.

---

```
top
```

### Post by iaculallad on 2008-07-07
One reason might be that you have a "heavyweight" xorg configuration file that's taking up much of your system's resources.

### Post by FredJones on 2008-07-07
> **vor said:**
> Do you have any applications open at all?  Things like firefox and gnome-system-monitor tend to upset Xorg and make it use abnormal amounts of CPU.  I would try logging in, bringing up a terminal and typing top.  Then opening apps one by one to see if you can figure out which one is making Xorg unhappy.

I login to Gnome and run top in a terminal and all is quiet. I click on the "System Monitor" icon which I put in the top panel and indeed you are right. While gnome-system-monitor itself is quiet, Xorg is not--it is using 30% of my CPU.

I close gnome-system-monitor and /usr/bin/X goes to 2%. compiz.real is #2 taking 1%. The only apps open are that terminal and this Firefox.

Oddly enough, even though I am just typing in this textarea and not doing anything else, sometimes Xorg and/or firefox jump up to 20% momentarily.

Now I open nautilus and then Thunderbird and then close Nautilus and Xorg jumps up to 15%. It goes back to settle around 2 or 3% which I find odd, but I suppose it's acceptable.

I started to actually use the machine now--I ran a download with synaptic and I was browsing and using Thunderbird. I checked top every so often and sometimes Xorg still jumps up to as much as 30%. I removed the system monitor from the top panel but Xorg is still the top app in top, using 5% of my CPU when I am just typing in firefox. Firefox is second with 4%.

Is this normal?

---

### Post by sdennie on 2008-07-07
> **FredJones said:**
> I login to Gnome and run top in a terminal and all is quiet. I click on the "System Monitor" icon which I put in the top panel and indeed you are right. While gnome-system-monitor itself is quiet, Xorg is not--it is using 30% of my CPU.

I close gnome-system-monitor and /usr/bin/X goes to 2%. compiz.real is #2 taking 1%. The only apps open are that terminal and this Firefox.

Oddly enough, even though I am just typing in this textarea and not doing anything else, sometimes Xorg and/or firefox jump up to 20% momentarily.

Now I open nautilus and then Thunderbird and then close Nautilus and Xorg jumps up to 15%. It goes back to settle around 2 or 3% which I find odd, but I suppose it's acceptable.

I started to actually use the machine now--I ran a download with synaptic and I was browsing and using Thunderbird. I checked top every so often and sometimes Xorg still jumps up to as much as 30%. I removed the system monitor from the top panel but Xorg is still the top app in top, using 5% of my CPU when I am just typing in firefox. Firefox is second with 4%.

Is this normal?

The gnome-system-monitor abusing Xorg is very normal and there is a bug filed about it.  The firefox behavior seems relatively normal and you'll also see Xorg jump to 100% when mousing over a lot of links in firefox.  A good example of that is to go the front page of these forums, scroll to the gigantic user list at the bottom and just mouse over the links.  I'm not sure about Thunderbird as I don't use it.

Essentially, they are bugs in the apps and annoying but, shouldn't have a huge impact on system performance.

### Post by philinux on 2008-07-08
I like system-monitor and the way to reduce it's xorg cpu usage is to make it's window smaller especially horizontally. I have mine set to half the screen.
The bad behavior only appears when you click the resources tab with a full screen.

---
